title: OpenAI Releases GPT-5.5, Its Smartest Model Yet
summary: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5, its most capable model to date, with major gains in agentic coding, scientific research, and computer use — and a step toward a unified AI 'super app'.
content: OpenAI released GPT-5.5 on April 23, calling it its 'smartest and most intuitive to use model' yet and a significant step toward a planned AI super app combining ChatGPT, Codex, and an AI browser into one unified service. President Greg Brockman described it as 'a faster, sharper thinker for fewer tokens compared to something like 5.4,' noting that the model advances toward more agentic and intuitive computing for both businesses and consumers. Chief Research Officer Mark Chen highlighted that GPT-5.5 shows meaningful gains on scientific and technical research workflows and could assist with drug discovery, while Chief Scientist Jakub Pachocki said the team expects 'extremely significant improvements in the medium term.' The model benchmarks higher than Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 across a range of evaluations, and has been classified as 'High' cybersecurity risk — capable of amplifying existing pathways to harm — prompting additional safeguards for API deployments. GPT-5.5 is rolling out to Plus, Pro, Business, and Enterprise ChatGPT users, with GPT-5.5 Pro available for the most demanding workloads.

title: OpenAI Brings Models to AWS After Restructuring Microsoft Exclusivity Deal
summary: OpenAI's models, including GPT-5.5 and the Codex coding agent, are now available on Amazon Bedrock after the company restructured its long-standing Microsoft exclusivity arrangement.
content: A day after OpenAI capped its revenue-share payments to Microsoft and gained the right to serve customers on any cloud, it announced that its models will be available through Amazon Web Services via Amazon Bedrock. AWS customers can access OpenAI's latest models, including GPT-5.5, as well as the Codex coding agent and a new Amazon Bedrock Managed Agents service powered by OpenAI that enables construction of sophisticated customized agents with memory of previous interactions. AWS CEO Matt Garman said at a San Francisco launch event, 'This is what our customers have been asking us for for a really long time.' OpenAI's revenue chief Denise Dresser had previously acknowledged that the Microsoft relationship 'has also limited our ability to meet enterprises where they are — for many that's Bedrock.' The expanded partnership comes on top of a $50 billion Amazon investment in OpenAI announced earlier this year, and OpenAI's commitment to use two gigawatts of AWS' custom Trainium chips for model training.

title: Google Grants Pentagon Full AI Access After Anthropic's Refusal
summary: Google has given the U.S. Department of Defense access to its AI for classified networks, stepping in after Anthropic refused similar terms and was branded a national security risk.
content: Google has granted the U.S. Department of Defense broad access to its AI for classified networks, essentially permitting all lawful uses, following a high-profile standoff in which Anthropic refused the DoD's demand for unrestricted AI access. The Pentagon had sought terms that would allow use of AI for domestic mass surveillance and autonomous weapons — conditions Anthropic rejected, leading the DoD to brand it a 'supply-chain risk,' a designation normally reserved for foreign adversaries. Anthropic and the DoD are now in active litigation, with a judge granting Anthropic an injunction last month while the case continues. Google becomes the third major AI company — after OpenAI and xAI — to sign a deal with the DoD, though its agreement includes non-binding language indicating it doesn't intend for its AI to be used in mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. The deal was struck despite an open letter signed by 950 Google employees urging the company to follow Anthropic's lead and insist on comparable guardrails.

title: NVIDIA Launches Nemotron 3 Nano Omni, a Unified Vision-Audio-Language Model
summary: NVIDIA unveiled Nemotron 3 Nano Omni, an open multimodal model that unifies vision, audio, and language into one system for AI agents, delivering up to 9x higher throughput than comparable open omni models.
content: NVIDIA's Nemotron 3 Nano Omni is a new open multimodal model built on a 30B-A3B hybrid mixture-of-experts architecture that eliminates the need for separate vision, speech, and language models within AI agent pipelines, cutting latency and fragmented context across modalities. The model achieves 9x higher throughput than other open omni models with the same interactivity, tops six leaderboards in complex document intelligence and video and audio understanding, and is already being adopted by companies including Palantir, Foxconn, and H Company. H Company's CEO noted that building on Nemotron 3 Nano Omni allows agents to 'rapidly interpret full HD screen recordings — something that wasn't practical before,' describing it as 'a fundamental shift in how our agents perceive and interact with digital environments in real time.' The model is released with open weights, datasets, and training techniques, and can be deployed anywhere from NVIDIA Jetson edge hardware to hyperscale data centers via Hugging Face, OpenRouter, and NVIDIA NIM microservices. The broader Nemotron 3 family has surpassed 50 million downloads in the past year, and Nano Omni extends it into full multimodal and agentic domains.

title: IBM Launches Bob, an AI Development Partner for the Full Software Lifecycle
summary: IBM launched IBM Bob, an AI-first development partner already used by 80,000+ IBM employees with a reported 45% average productivity gain, now available as a SaaS product for enterprise teams.
content: IBM announced the global availability of IBM Bob, an agentic AI platform that goes beyond code completion to orchestrate the full software development lifecycle — from planning, coding, and testing through deployment, modernization, and operations. Bob uses multi-model orchestration to automatically route tasks to the right model based on accuracy, latency, and cost, drawing on frontier models including Anthropic Claude, Mistral, and IBM Granite alongside specialized fine-tuned models for code reasoning and security. Piloted inside IBM since June 2025, Bob is now used by over 80,000 IBM employees, with surveyed users reporting an average 45% productivity gain; the Instana team reported a 70% reduction in time on selected tasks, and Blue Pearl completed a typical 30-day Java upgrade in just 3 days using Bob. Real-world customers include Ernst & Young, which is using Bob to modernize its global tax platform, and APIS IT, which achieved 10x faster architecture analysis on mainframe and .NET modernization projects. IBM Bob is now generally available as a SaaS offering with a complimentary 30-day trial, and on-premises deployment is planned for organizations with data residency requirements.

title: 1 in 4 S&P 500 Companies Can Now Prove AI Pays
summary: New data shows 25% of S&P 500 companies reported at least one quantifiable AI business impact in Q1 2026, nearly double the share from a year earlier, signaling AI's shift from pilot to production.
content: One in four S&P 500 companies reported a quantifiable AI business impact in Q1 2026, up from 13% in Q1 2025, with tech leading at 42% and finance surging from 15% to 40% — the steepest single-year climb of any non-tech sector. The pace of change has been striking: in August 2025, 98% of chief product officers at billion-dollar companies were unwilling to grant autonomous agents any real authority, but by November active AI deployment had reached 23% and nearly 40% of enterprise product leaders had given agents real access to business-critical systems. Morgan Stanley analysts expect 74–89% of AI-related benefits over the next 12–24 months to come from cost efficiency rather than revenue growth, with companies concentrating investment in workflow automation and back-office processes where ROI is easiest to measure. The readiness gap remains significant: 71% of senior technology executives say their own organization — not the technology — limits AI performance, with 63% citing data quality as the top barrier and only 15% saying their data environments are mostly integrated across the company. The overall picture reflects a clear shift from AI as a pilot to AI as a production asset, with the competitive question moving from 'whether to adopt' to 'how fast to execute.'
